speaker: interviewer
text: Nathan, when you were talking to me about your family before, you mentioned your grandsons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have two grandsons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me their names and how old they are--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anything you want to say about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one, the name is Eric. His bar mitzvah was two years-- I think two years-- yeah, two years ago, by 13. He's now 15 years old. He goes to a private school. Very nice boy, very tall-- tallest as I am. And the younger boy is going to be 15 next-- yeah, he's 15. No, the younger boy is going to be bar mitzvahed. He's 12. Next year is going to be his bar mitzvah, the younger boy. His name is Brian. And my daughter's name is Miriam. She was born-- I think I told you already-- March 12, 1951. My son-in-law's name is Lee Green, G-R-E-E-N. And he's a doctor, hematology and oncology. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- the grandson that was bar mitvahed two years ago--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was that like for you to be at his bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was-- well, it was-- it would be much better if I would have my wife there, too. She wasn't in there. She wasn't with us. But it was-- it was-- sure, it's a happy occasion, our son-- our grandson, a bar mitzvah. But you know how it is. I didn't smile. It was-- I felt very bad. Because it's-- they celebrate and everything, and you couldn't see my smile on my face, not because I don't like it something, just-- just-- the-- itself-- my wife-- I am over there enjoying a bar mitzvah, and she's lying there and away from everything. So it's a very bad feeling. But like this, it's all right. I mean, I'm glad I have them over here. Because-- because in case of something happened, I have somebody to call. It's my family. My daughter, you see, they have their own problems. But any time-- any time I need them, they are here, 12 o'clock at night, whenever. A lot of things-- I came over here, what I couldn't arrange by myself. So they are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you came over here, you mean when you came from Brooklyn--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Brooklyn to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Los Angeles in last three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They helped me a lot. Well, she rented apartment for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and like this, I have still friends in New York. I-- I have calls almost every day, almost every night from New York-- very close friends. They're still-- most of them-- she has-- Regina, she has an aunt. Her uncle's wife is still alive in-- in Brooklyn. And I have some friends, but they all already in the age. And a lot of them, they-- they feel the age, you see. I, thank God, feel good. I'm not a youngster, either. But I-- all my life I was-- for instance, when I was a small kid already, a little bit older, I used to go bicycle riding. I used to play soccer. And over here, since I am in Brooklyn, over here in Brooklyn I used to belong to Jack LaLanne, go every day after work, step for swimming and exercises. The same thing over here-- for two years I used to belong to Family Fitness Center on Pico. Now I belong to JCC, Jewish Community Center.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I enjoy it a lot. I feel-- I feel more home over here. I go swimming. And all-- all-- for instance, they ask me, what are you doing when you go to the exercises? The doctor told me, what are you doing when you go for exercise? I told her, everything the youngsters are doing, I am doing. [LAUGHING] Good for you. Good for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you were to-- if your grandsons were sitting with you here right now, and you wanted to give them a message, and a message to the world as well, 50 years after the Holocaust--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what would your message be to them and to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would be wanting-- live while you are young. Because you never know what tomorrow going to be. That's the message to everybody. Life is unpredictable. You never know what tomorrow going to be. So while you're young, you have a chance, live. Because I was waiting. When I was young, I couldn't take advantage of my life because I was in the concentration camp. When the war was finished, after the concentration camps, I have to look how to survive. You have no friends. Nobody take care of you. You have to live, to build something to exist, just to exist. Then when I was working-- then when I was working, I was young, young-- still I was working. I made good money and working as a tool- and die-maker in-- in New York. It was good money. So I saved, saved, saved. I think I retire, I'm going to live a little bit. Meantime, it turns out different. You retire, always something happened. The money, what I saved while I was younger working, I'm spending now on her. In life, no matter-- I can't live now, because whenever I go, I have her on my mind. I'm enjoying myself over here, and she's laying there and crying, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's up to the individual. A lot of people-- I see other people, no matter what it is, they do not care. They enjoy their lives. I can't. So the best thing is while you're young, take advantage of your young years, of your health, and live. Thank you for that. I know that you brought-- you have some pictures that-- and some documents that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the war time and--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the liberation and from-- from a few of my friends, from my wife. From my father's wife, from my wife's father, her stepmother, I have pictures. And I have some pictures from the place-- from the people where I was hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold that, and we're going to-- because I want you to get-- I want you to be able to get the pictures so we can see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'll stop for a minute so that we can do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- My name is-- I am over here in that picture on the right side, the tallest, the tallest on that picture. Should show it? No. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell us where it was, where the picture is.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was in the place where I was born, Biecz, Poland. It was taken, I think, in 1920. Used to go to the Jewish school, Talmud Torah. It says over here on the bottom in Hebrew, Talmud Torah [INAUDIBLE], children, what they call today a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still alive from these children-- could I show you? Because the-- date of portrait-- the date of portrait could be made in-- made in 1927. I don't remember exactly when.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's alive, from this-- all that children is alive. Two brothers, who were twins-- two twins-- they are over here. This one and this one, they were killed in the Israeli Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two kids on the bottom in the front row-- the sixth-- what it is? One two, three, four, five-- the sixth and the seventh are killed in the Israeli Army after the war. A few-- the second row-- one, two, three, four-- the fifth-- the second row, the fifth from the left is in Israel alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Naftali-- Naftali [? Ray ?] is in Israel. And then another brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the third row from the top to your right is in French Paris. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the rest of them is not alive. Some of them, they died-- I can't tell exactly which ones. Some of them are died in Israel after the war. Last year, two years ago, that-- this is only about four of us are still alive from that picture. Yeah. This is the liberation paper from the concentration camp in 1945 from the-- liberated May 6, 2 o'clock in the afternoon by the American Army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is from the-- I was interviewed by the CI-- this was 1945. Exact day, I don't remember. In 1945, I was interviewed by the CIA, the reason why I was in concentration camp. And the only reason why I was in concentration camp was because I was Jewish. But over here you have everything, the date of birth and-- from 1942 to 1945, concentration camp, Mauthausen in Krakow-Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After the liberation, two weeks after the liberation-- I met-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met an American-- what was the day written on the top? What is? A platoon Sergeant.

speaker: interviewee
text: The platoon-- he was an officer from that platoon. And he took me-- he asked me if I could work in a garage. And I work in the garage by the American, but that platoon there-- for, I don't know, a month long or for something like-- from [INAUDIBLE], 11 July, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: 11th of July, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I was working-- from May 1920 to July 12, I worked by them in the garage and eat with the Army and slept there with them. And then they moved up Army to Japan. It still was-- the war still was going on with Japan.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I want to go back to Poland to look for my family. And I left that-- that-- army unit, and they gave me-- that-- that officer. What was his name? Fischer or something, very nice man. Franklin Fischer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Franklin Fischer. And he gave me a paper that every American truck or car should-- if they should pick me up and take me all the way to the-- to the Czechoslovakian border. And I went-- from there I went to Czechoslovakia, from Czechoslovakia to Poland. And I came to Poland and find out that everything is gone. My brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this picture, this is-- to my left, this is my brother, Szja Fleischer. He was born, I think, in 1912. And to the right is my sister, Helen Fleischer. She was born, I think, in 1914. I don't know exactly. They both not alive. My sister-- my brother died. My brother was in concentration camp and-- Flossenburg-- Flossenburg concentration camp-- and died three days after the war was finished. And my sister went together with my parents. This, I know. I don't know exactly when they-- where they finished all. Place-- in Biecz, Poland, where I was born. When I came back-- I left [INAUDIBLE]. And where she-- later, where they took her, where they kill her, I don't know. Date I left-- I left-- wait a minute, wait a minute. Wait. It's so different to remember. When do I go to-- the pictures of my brother and sister was taken in 1936 in Biecz, Poland. To the left, it's myself, Nathan Fleischer. This-- this-- the second to me, when he was in the concentration camps with us, he was 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ira Goetz. He's now a professor from languages. He lives in New York. Next to him is alive, is from the same town, Biecz, from where I come, is Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. He's living now in Florida. To-- all the way to your right is a uncle from that-- from that boy, Mr. Goetz. He's not alive. He died, I don't know, 10 years ago. He's not alive. This was taken-- that picture was taken in 1947 in Germany, Bamburg. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my wife and myself, a picture of my wife and myself, taken in 1940-- I think in 1949, in Bamburg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Regina Fleischer. Maiden name is Regina [INAUDIBLE]. She was born in Tarnow, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, this picture is my wife, Regina Fleischer, and myself, Nathan Fleischer. It was taken in 1950 in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm sorry, I [INAUDIBLE] with the date of birth-- I mean, of the date when that picture was maken-- was maken in 1985, was the picture maken, in Brooklyn, New York. This is my daughter, Mariam Green, and her husband, Lee Green, made in Los Angeles, California in 1992. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandson, Eric Green, and his brother, Brian Green. The picture was made in his bar mitzvah in 1992 in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture from a Polish woman who, during the war in 1942, I was hiding in Poland by her father. This is the picture from myself, to the left, and her. Her name is [? Swafka ?] [? Wabetska. ?] Next to her is my wife, Regina. And next to her is her grandchildren, two grandchildren, and her daughter. They live now in Canada. I think Winnipeg, Canada. And they came to visit me in Brooklyn, New York, 1990. I think 1990. I don't remember exactly.
